text: The cultural ADD diagnosis carries a class dimension that is rarely acknowledged. Access to long-form reading — novels, essays, longform journalism — correlates strongly with socioeconomic status, as does the ability to work in distraction-free environments. Jonathan Crary's "24/7" argues that the relentless stimulation of digital commerce is not equally distributed: those with greater resources purchase silence, sequestration, and depth, while attention-harvesting platforms are most aggressively monetized in low-income markets. The collapse of collective attention capacity is therefore not merely cultural but stratified — deepening a cognitive divide that maps onto existing inequalities.
question: What argument does Jonathan Crary's '24/7' make about the distribution of digital stimulation?

options.text: That digital distraction is a universal condition affecting all economic classes equally
That attention-harvesting platforms are most aggressively monetized in low-income markets
That long-form reading has been replaced by video content across all demographics
That economic inequality is caused primarily by differences in media consumption habits
